chunks.text: G'day. Why?

Hello. Hey, staff, why has my name changed to Greg McCallum? Just wondering, is that you, Laurie? Sorry, we'll change that. Bye. Thank you very much. Much to the amusement of Cameron. Thank you. Right. Have we got our next submitter, the Canterbury Mayoral Forum?
G'day, Steve. G'day. How are you? Good, good.

Now, we're... This is A, is it, or B? B. B. Great. I'm in the right place then. Okay. Okay, looks like we've got the Canterbury Mayoral Forum now. Oh, up now. Yes, you have. Hello, everybody. Yeah, tena koutou katoa. My name- Okay. G'day. Well, I'll just... I'll just quickly introduce myself. I'm the, uh-... Grant Callum, the chair of the, of the, of the subcommittee of the Environmental Committee.

Yeah. We have 15 minutes with you today. Um, we have your submission. Feel free to take it as read, and, it'd be great to leave a bit of time for questions. A couple of my other colleagues are still coming online, but fire away. Okay. Well, good m- good afternoon to you all. Um, my name is Marie Black, and I'm the mayor of the Hurunui District, and also the deputy chair of the Canterbury Mayoral Forum, and I give my apologies to, Mayor Nigel Bowen, who's unable to be with us today. But I also have with me Hamish Dobbie, who is the chair of the

Canterbury Chief Executives Forum, and he's the CE from the Hurunui District Council. So we're both here together. Um, so thank you for your time today, and I will take the submission as read, but I'll just take you through some points that, we believe that, endorse the thinking of Canterbury Mayoral Forum. Um, it'll be no surprise to you all that we have a strong and proud record of working together, for the good of the whole region, and, we are concerned of course about the raft of, changes are being proposed with

the, with, with the reform packages. Um, we can't understate, the implementation costs of the RMA, reaching something like $10 bill- billion, and, that is borne by ratepayers. So they... There are some overall concerns that we have, but, we ins- we do support the intent of the change. Um, in regard to mana whenua and the treaty settings, the Mayoral Forum, does not support the change from the general provision that requires decision-makers to have regard to the principle of the Treaty of Waitangi.

Um, w- we believe that that does risk the undermining of partnerships that we already have in place with, our local mana whenua, and that we believe that we have, significant benefit, by working closely with, our local Ngāi Tahu, and that we have got, um... In the past have, have worked...

those relationships, have delivered, faster at a lower cost and reduced the litigation that when the strong partnership approaches are implemented, implemented and that, I think Environment Canterbury has also spoken to the US Select Committee in that regard.Um, I'll just take you through the implementation and transition.

So the timeline, we believe from a Canterbury Mayoral Forum was, extremely ambitious, and that it will create substantial resourcing and capability challenges for many councils, particularly if we look at that in regard to the other reform proposals that are in front of us. Um, and the reforms, of course, will require significant investment of people capability and ca- capacity. This investment, should not be underestimated. Um, if we look at it from a spatial plan perspective, the mayoral sub- mayoral forum supports the proposal to develop concise and consistent

national direct policy direction, and, uh... but we need to make sure that the system will be, cost effective and efficient, and must be in place before local authorities commence development of their spatial plans. Um, sorry, I'm just taking you through the bullet points here.

Um, the mayoral forum, the bill should provide for local authorities significant discretion to decide how spatial planning committees will be formed, and, that we believe that it could place some challenge, polit- it could be politically challenging and slow in a large region such as Canterbury. Um, the mayoral forum does not support the minister being the final decision maker on areas that a region cannot agree on. Um, when we're thinking about the regulatory relief, as drafted, we have a number of adverse impacts

for our councils and our ratepayers and our communities, and we're looking for a greater, to qualify some of those impacts from a financial perspective. Um, adding that to the rate capping de- debate, it may deter, councils from protecting important areas because they're not in a position to provide suitable relief.

Um, and I guess just in closing before we open, you may have some questions around that, we as the Canterbury Mayoral Forum have been in a strong, position and a strong advocate for subsidiarity and locally led decision making. Uh, local government's role in the community must be reflected in the resource management system.

Um, we are particularly concerned about the shift towards greater centralized decision making. As currently proposed, the new system, dilutes the role of elected members as advocates and decision makers for their communities, and results in a lack of community voice. Uh, we do not support the system where more decisions are made through spatial plans and combined plans, but it is critical that communities can see themselves in these processes.

Canterbury is a large, diverse region, and within the region its community's sense of place and identity for urban and rural is fundamental to its ongoing success. Regulations when thresholds are set appropriately and tested through evidence promotes growth, enabling and shaping great cities, townships and neighborhoods, and I'm sure that that's what you're all seeking as far as the, the reform package goes. Um, and we, and we want to have those environments set where which compliments and enhances their identity, and it's something we

fundamentally do not want to lose. So, that was a whistle stop tour of, of our, written submission that you have in front of you and you have read, and, I guess that if there are questions around that, we would be happy to receive them. Thank you.

Thank you. I really appreciate that. Right, well, first question to Cameron Luxton. Oh, thank you, Chair. Um, thank you to the mayoral forum. Now, sorry that I'm only gonna focus on, three areas which don't include Hurunui, but, I'd take this opportunity to ask you about the Canterbury Earthquake Recovery Act, and how changes to the Local Government Act and the RMA from 2011 helped, the recovery, help, and Christchurch City become, you know, the place that it's looking like today.

I know it only affected Christchurch, Selwyn and Waimakariri, but do you have a, a, a view on the, the way that bill and, and what that did, contributed to the recovery as it's seen today? Thanks, Cameron. Hamish, did you want to do that? Uh, I can have a crack at it. Um, yeah, look, I think, think that, act, really worked as a partnership between local and central government, and, did enable things to be done quicker, and, and got some of the red tape out of the way so that things could get on and happen.

So I think it was, it was a pretty important enabling act to, to, fundamental to the recovery of Christchurch. And do you see any of the lessons from that reflected in these bills? Uh, yes. We, we, I think all agree in the Canterbury Mayoral Forum that, anything that, lessens some of the process and red tape around consenting is good for, economic growth, so long as appropriate, checks and balances are in place.

Thank you. Uh, Steve Aymond- Is Rachael, can I sup on that? Just a little- Sure, sure. By the way, Rachael, you'll sup. Go for it. Yep. Yeah. Did you also, make comment on the Natural and Built Environments Act? And would you agree that that also reduced a lot of process?

Uh, yes. We, we would. I mean, we didn't a- actually get to see it in action, but, but it looked like it would've, done, done some of the same things as, contemplated hereUm, so we see some parallels between the two. Um, broadly we see this being slightly more enabling, but that's just a matter of degree, I think.

Uh, Steve Adams, his question. Right. Thank you. Uh, I got a couple, if that's all right. But the, um- That's all right, yeah... the first one is, you speak on the impact of regulatory relief on the ability of you to fulfill the stated bill's objective. Uh, on point 64, you think the significant fiscal compensation becomes, associated with regulatory protection methods.

Councils may rationally retreat from these methods, resulting in diminished protection of environmental and heritage values. This outcome would undermine the bill's stated objectives. Can you kind of expand on the fundamental problem of regulatory relief actually undermining, the purported intention of the bill to protect the environment?

Yep. Uh, certainly. Cath, do you wanna- Yeah. No, you go. Um, so regulatory relief comes at a, at a cost to councils, and ultimately that's a cost to ratepayers in the di- in the district. So councils may choose to make trade-offs, between rates increases to fund that regulatory relief and the desired level of protection that we're, would wish to achieve, for, for, heritage or environmental features in the district. So, um- Yeah... it'll come to a straight economic trade-off, we believe, or potentially believe, and things like rates capping will make that trade-off much more

stark. I'll just have a sup on that if you don't mind, Steve. Um, the... Just, so surely the... I mean, it's all, you know... I mean, I get it, you, there's a potential cost for councils. But the flip side is for landowners, if you, if you, if you impose, regulations on them, whether it's SNAs or, outstanding natural landscapes and so forth, and it has a significant impact on their business or the value of their property, surely those people are entitled to some relief, from it, and w- which would then mean, I would've thought that the actual decisions you make at, are ones that

community really value, because obviously the community is, which is gonna benefit, will then have to pay, will be paying for it. Uh, some, some of these things, will be, will be driven by, national standards or national policy statements, and if that's the case, then maybe the government should be paying for them rather than the ratepayers within a district.

Well, that would depend on what the standards are, of course. Righto. Uh, Steve. Yeah. The other point you made, eloquently already is around the impact of removing consideration of Te Tiriti o Waitangi. Um, I wonder, it, it strikes me that this seems to be fixing a problem that doesn't exist or rather undermining and hampering, the beneficial way of working you currently have with hapū and iwi, and which is also consistent with upholding the treaty, and that seems to be a fundamentally, undermining thing to be doing to, the progress we've made in this

country with that relationship. I wonder if you can reflect on the positive aspect of that relationship and why the current wording may damage that. Oh, I'm just going to say, you know, the, the, the foundation of relationships actually builds and builds the bridges that, from our past, you know.

And, I think that mayoral forum supports the, the fundamental of having that relationship in the, in the district, you know, that you live and primarily on the, with the roanga that you need to be working with in that regard. Yeah. And you're saying there's mutual benefits there that, you know, you see it as a fundamentally beneficial thing for everybody.

Yes. That's correct. And, and, and just as a, as a sup on that, please, because I mean, that's interesting compared to like I talk to a number of farmers in the, in the area in fact, and, and when they go through consenting processes, they, you know, the, you know, the, the, the right to farm and carry on their businesses- Mm... they're feeling it's, they, they feel that the relationship at that, at that level with iwi is, putting a lot of stress on them working through the process, and is also leading to some very short term

consents. Um, and, and it be... And so how do you marry those two things up? Uh, yeah, it's a good point to raise. Um, I guess if I can drill that back to the Herenui district, I know I'm speaking on behalf of mayoral forum, but, the development of the relationship with our roanga, has probably supported and, and enabled. But, you know, we're not, we're not there yet.

We need to continue to work on that to build those relationships to get the, the desirable outcome that we're seeking. Okay. Thank you. Look, our time is up, so thank you very much for yours. I really appreciate it. Uh, appreciate your submission on behalf of the mayoral forum, and, have a great day. Thank you. Thank you. Thank you.
Okay. Our next submitter is Tauranga City. Waiti submission we have here from them. I understand they've just come on now. Um,

Tauranga City. Are we, are we... Is it Tauranga, not Te Kahu? Hold on. Who's next? Let me have a look. Oh, sorry, Te Kahu. Yeah, Te Kahu, I took... My fault, my, my bad. Thank you. Now just I need to understand, please, Huhana, Steve, which of you is actually here representing the Green Party this time? You, Huhana.

It's Huhana here. Okay. I'm representing the Greens in this one. Okay. I'll sit and then listen. That's fine. Thanks, mate. Just so I understand. Thank you. Righto. Bye, Phil. Uh, righto. Okay. Look, welcome. Um, welcome to, Te Kahu A- A- Atonui. Um, great to have you here.

Uh, w- we've had... Your submission has been received. Feel free to take it as read. Highlight a few points, and you got 15 minutes of our time far away. Thank you Oh, kia ora tātou. Um, ko Phil Grimshaw tōku ingoa. Uh, tēnei au ki te kaitohutohu, kai o or senior advisor to Te Kahu o Taonui.

Um, unfortunately, our co-chairs were unavailable this morning due to some of their scheduling issues, so, apologies from Katie Murray and Apirana Edwards. No worries. Yeah. So, also, you know, like you said, Grant, we'll please take our submission as read. I'd like to pick up on some, some key details, if you don't mind.

Yeah. Fire away. Thank you. So, Te Kahu o Taonui, we're, we're not opposed to economic development, bearing in mind the historical relationship that we have with Te Whakaputanga and Te Tiriti o Waitangi, which for all intents and purposes were, were mechanisms of how we reasserted our rangatiratanga or sovereignty and the authority to support our communities and generations to come, whilst obviously maintaining our relationship with te taiao, the environment.

Te Kahu o Taonui is not opposed-- oh, sorry, we are opposed to the bills as they are currently drafted, because they will continue to marginalize our rights and interests, and they will leave a legacy that will impact on our generations to come. From our perspective, this is a deliberate strategy of this national coalition-led government when it is put into the context via some of the legislative changes, specifically to Māori wards, disestablishment of Te Aka Whai Ora, the Māori Health Authority, changes to smoke-free

legislation, repealing Section 7AA of the Oranga Tamariki Act, amendments to the Takutai Mana Act and macro-applications processes, as well as repealing the hierarchical obligations of Te Mana o te Wai and reviewing treaty clauses. So if you look at that in, in, in its context, we feel that this marginalization is, is also reinforced by the pace and speed of this government's reforms, with little to no consultation or engagement with your treaty partners.

And if I can just provide some examples, if we look at information from your officials specifically have highlighted these areas concerned. So if we look at the Fast Track Amendment Bill, and some of the information, provided by your officials was due to timeframes and limited opportunity to garner the views of treaty partners on the specific policy proposals, this treaty impact analysis was unable to be detailed or as thorough as would be expected for a bill of this significance. Similarly, if we're looking at RMA or

RM Bill 1, in light of the limited engagement and uncertain impact of the proposal on freshwater, it was difficult to assess whether or not the treaty principles of partnership and active protection have been met, whether or not general engagement obligations contained in some treaty settlements have been met, whether or not processes provided for in certain settle-settlements have been met, implications for the Crown's commitment on Māori freshwater rights and interests. Due to the limited time available, it is-- You get the drift. So

you can probably understand why in the context of these bills, we can, you can understand how we feel, feeling marginalized in this process. As treaty partners, we do not see that this government is acting in good faith, and we do not support the narrowing of your treaty responsibilities within these bills.

So focusing on your treaty obligations just with our post-settlement governance entities marginalizes those iwi and hapū within Tai Tokerau have, that haven't under-undertaken this process and gone through it yet. So and that's another key factor for us.

One of the causal factors for the reform was around the RMA delivering poor environmental outcomes, and there is enough data that clearly identifies that our ecosystem and environmental indicators are getting worse, not better. I mean, all you need to do is look at the State of the Environment report. When was the first one? 1997?

And if you look at some of the indicators that are currently being, provided by your ministry at the minute, things are getting worse, they're not getting better. So for Te Kahu o Taonui, this highlights the importance of maintaining Part 2 matters, specifically Sections 6C, 7A, and 8, and our ability to support environmental protection outcomes, which includes the role of mātauranga Māori or traditional ecological knowledge that provides data and expertise at place.

These bills are unashamedly pro-development, and from Te Kahu o Taonui's perspective, they do not have enough environmental protections. We should be actively restoring our ecosystems rather than focusing exclusively on development outcomes. Finally, from a Te Kahu o Taonui perspective, w-we have consistently articulated that using the select committee process to engage and consult with your treaty partner lacks no mana, and it's a really, really poor approach to lawmaking.

In conclusion, thank you very much for the opportunity for us to orally present, and, I'm o-opening up for any questions. Uh, thank you. Uh, Huhana has your hand up for a question. Tena koe, Phil. Otira tena koutou te Kahu o Taonui. Uh, for those that don't know who Te Kahu o Taonui are, they represent the iwi collective of Tai Tokerau, and so I want to acknowledge you as our technician, Phil, coming on board to present the shared collective view of the iwi of Tai Tokerau.

A collective which is a mixture of settled and unsettled, but also knowing that the RMA, really the rubber hits the road in marae, kainga, and in hapū. So-Knowing that you've been in the game for a really long time, Matua Phil, you've already outlined how, how hard it's been to even see ourselves in the legislation, and the fact that largely iwi have come forward and said, "They've been ignored. There has been no engagement with us in the developing of this legislation." What would be the corrective action that you would

recommend to our select committee? Because you, you pointed out, iwi are being treated as Joe Public, and are being herded into this public select committee process instead of coming in at the devi- design stage of, legislation. Should we pause and, look to regroup, take on board this feedback, and mahi tahi with tangata whenua, and what could be a, a system for resource management that honors Te Tiriti o Waitangi?

Oh, kia ora. Oh, kia ora, Huhana. Listen, thank you very much for your patai. Uh, the short answer is absolutely. I mean, we've all been in different fora when you've got people at the table working collaboratively, and, and whilst you may agree and, and disagree on matters, you're actually working through what they look like kanohi ki te kanohi, or face to face.

When you've got a process similar to this and similar to the other legislative amendments that have been coming through from this national coalit- coalition-led government, you can understand the frustration. And for those that aren't at the table, then it becomes really, really hard to provide some really helpful support and opportunities around what that might look like. So a- as an example, will, will scientific data provide the environmental outcomes that, that, that we require and we need as a community? There's a significant amount of anecdotal

information coming from right across our communities, both our whanau, hapu, marae level community groups, and it's that type of information that actually bolsters the opportunity for us to, to be involved. And I think if we're choosing to have a speed dating process around RM reform, I, I, I think it's gonna come back and, and bite us intergenerationally.

Can I, can I, sup on that, Mr. Chair? Yeah, yeah. Sure, fire away. Fire away. Um, so what I've seen and what I've heard is that there's, like, a new hierarchy in terms of te Māori and how Māori enter the system and have their voices heard, from a perspective of te kāhui tauno, 'cause, the primary voice now seems to be those that are settled.

Um, and yet we know in the kainga, the marae and the hapu are the ones who are activating and do the work on the ground often. Um, the narrowing also where we see Mana Whakahauna Aroha have a very short timeframe now to exist. Are these all ways where it just creates new grievance am- amongst Māori in a resource management system that could be something more fulsome and inclusive?

Absolutely, Huhana. I mean, you know, there's a, a lot of narrative and rhetoric around it's because of Māori and our cultural values and our relationship with, te taiao that, that we're limiting or we're not supportive of development, and that, like I said, w- we, we support economic development, but not at the expense of degrading our ecosystems.

I mean, from any type of natural resource management philosophy, that is crazy. Maybe I can give you an example around something off. Um, we're currently going through a process around rock lobster. Our hapu and our whanau and marae, especially gatherers when it comes to tangihanga and, and hui, have clearly articulated that the populations are getting worse, yet the system necessarily hasn't picked up on, on what that looks like moving forward.

And if you think about it, the Fisheries Act is around sustainable utilization, so it's a, it's an economic development piece of legislation. Um, and if we maintain a pro-development stance within the RMA, with the reform of the RMA, then we're gonna get similar crashes within our systems.

And, and some of those cultural health indicators are currently expressing that right now, right now, whether it's water quality, whether it's water quantity. So we've got all these allocation issues with freshwater coming down catchments. There, there's significant amount of issues happening at us, and, and I think marginalizing where, Māori and wada communities participate in this space, if they can participate at all, is seriously flawed from our perspective.

Okay. Okay, so there, well, that's-- any other further questions? I've got one if there's time. No, no, sorry. Huhana's speaking on your behalf on this select committee, so it, yep. My bad. I'll stop. Yeah. No, no worries, Steve. Okay. Um, okay. Thank you. Okay, well, thanks very much, Phil, for your, submission. Really appreciate it.

Um, if you- Sorry, sorry. Grant, could I just, ask a que- Wanna wind up? Yeah, sure. You're our MP Yeah Do you have any questions of, of me in this space? I, I'm, I've been listening carefully. I've read your submission, and I take on board what you're saying. But, you know, I, I do... The one thing I would say is that, being a, a, a f- a farmer, a person of the land, I mean, we all, yeah, we all care about our environment and trying to improve it.

And things are definitely... We've all acknowledged there's been things happen in the past which need improving, and that's what we're focusing on, and that's what the bill's... Well, and it's about getting the balance right between, as you said, economic development and looking after the environment. So that's, that's where I sit and the way I approach it. And, and the communities do have a pa- opportunity as part of the consenting processes to contribute to, if you're applying for a consent to do things. Um, and your rock locks example's, an

interesting one. Um, it's, you know, it's, little bit... It's, it... And that's, and actually there are changes being proposed to that act to allow the minister to act a lot quicker because unfortunately in that case, it was, it... The, the way it, it was structured, they couldn't act quick enough, and, uh...

Which is, which is not, not acceptable. And so we're gonna look to improve that. So, yeah. Thank you. Thank, thank, thank you for that. Thanks, Phil. Cheers, eh. Okay. You didn't like to add before we, we wind up? Yeah. Sorry. Sorry, Phil? Yeah. Um, thank you for the opportunity.

I mean, you are our MP, so it'd be really good to see you engaging more with Te Kauhanganui on, on some of these key matters so that we're not necessarily having to going through a select committee process to be, to be heard. I, I think there's better ways that we could manage our relationship within Tai Tokerau. Okay. And I always happy to talk, Phil. No problem with that at all. Catch up.

Thank you. Appreciate the opportunity, the invitation. Ka kite. Okay. Thank you. Right. So we'll move on to... Now we have the Tauranga City Council. Got that wrong before. Okay. So, 'cause I just want me to get this from... Uh, right. So, right. Well, welcome everybody. Welcome to the, select committee this afternoon, Environment Select Committee. We have, 15 minutes with you.

We look forward to your presentation. We have, got your, submission, which please take as read, but feel free to high- to highlight a few points and then, then, leave, leave room for questions would be great. Fire away. Thank you. As mentioned, thank you very much for the opportunity for TCC to speak to this submission. I'm Councillor Roseboom, and with me I have Andrew Mead, head of City Growth and Infrastructure, Carl Luca, team leader, Structure Planning, and also in the room is Councillor Rod Taylor. We would like to provide you our support for these resource

management reforms. The RMA is simply broken, and it's not serving the needs of New Zealand. In Tauranga it presents a major impediment to enabling sufficient development capacity to meet current and future urban growth requirements in our high growth environment. In particular, we support having standardized provisions to provide efficiency in the system and consistency in decisions, and in the funnel approach with them narrowing down through national instruments, regional spatial plans, and combined regional plans. You've heard many submitters raise

concerns with aspects of the bill, such as regulatory relief framework, iwi participation, transition timeframes, and how these reforms are likely to be complicated by other reforms such as local government reforms, water services, and rates capping. Therefore, we do not see it necessary to speak to all these in detail, but we do share these concerns and emphasize the need for these to be addressed to enable timely, workable reforms.

I'd like to now pass to Andrew and Carl to speak to some of the specific technical concerns which we have raised in our submission. Thank you. Kia ora. Uh, my name's Carl Luca. I'm, yeah, team leader of Structure Planning and, and, involved in spatial planning. Uh, so I'm just gonna talk through a couple points on the regional spatial planning. Um, so overall, Tauranga City Council is very supportive of preparation of regional spatial plans, to integrate with the pl- integrate the planning bill and the natural environment

bill, and inform land use and natural environment plans. Uh, Tauranga City Council is part of Western Bay of Plenty Smart Growth, so which comprises representatives from each of our local authorities, Tangata Whenua, and central government, and we work in partnership to su- set a strategic vision and direction for growth in our subregion, the Western Bay of Plenty.

Uh, we've undertaken significant spatial planning for the subregion over the past 20 plus years, and that includes recently endorsing our Smart Growth strategy 2024 to '74, which includes, a future developments, strategy. Um, so we're seeking amendments to the planning bill to ensure that a subregional approach can be taken as part of the wider regional spatial planning process.

Um, Bay of Plenty is made up of several subregions, which are all quite different. Uh, the Western Bay, we have very high growth pressures, while some of our neighbors, Rotorua and the Eastern Bays, don't face these pressures as much. Um, and providing a subregional approach to spatial planning as part of that regional spatial planning process, so still working with our wider regional partners, will have advantages of stronger focus on local context, infrastructure priorities, and growth needs. Uh, it'll also tie in nicely, with the approach to

developing land use plans. Uh, to support timely delivery, national direction should clearly define what's in and out of first generation regional spatial plans. Uh, allowing a staged approach with improvement plans, identifying areas for f- further work will help with im- implementation in that first generation of regional spatial plans.And enabling regional spatial plans to, to build on work to date, to build on spatial planning to date, such as our endorsed smart growth strategy and future development strategy will also assist in a timely

rollout. Uh, last point on the regional spatial plans. Uh, we support prioritising a move to the new system alongside clear transitional mechanisms that will continue to enable development during that transitional period. Uh, in particular, the ability to rely on existing future development strategies and also come into play on regional spatial plans during that transition period and before the land use plan has been fully developed and, and completed. Um, and we're also very open to working with government on other transitional tools to allow that, development

to continue. Uh, I'm just gonna pass on to Andy to complete our sub. Yeah, hi there. Um, I was gonna touch on the national instruments. So the system that is proposed relies heavily on national instruments to provide directions to council, councils on the preparation of plans, what that standardised content will be, and how various resource management matters, that conflict with each other will be resolved. So we've had, quite a bit of experience in working through these with MFE

staff, and other government agencies, including on the national policy statements for, highly productive land and freshwater management. And to be honest, we've, we've often found this to be a significant challenge and for, I guess those staff in, in Wellington who are, you know, have got good intentions, they're not fully appreciating the issues that are experienced on the ground. So as such, from our perspective, it's essential that the national, instruments in the new system, and particularly the first ones that are required to inform our regional spatial

plans, our land use plans, and national natural environment plans, are, are subject to formal engagement, submission consultation type processes to ensure they're gonna be fit for purpose and workable right from the outset. Um, and that implementers of the system such as council, you know, we feel that we're well placed to identify issues, and find, yeah, workable resolutions to those. So that, that's a key point for us. Just moving on to, I guess the functions of the new system, and particularly the distinction between

the functions of, regional councils and territorial authorities. Um, we support having those, those, those clear boundaries. At the moment, there seems to be quite a bit of overlap between what councils do. However, we think there, there remains some uncertainties and complexities on where, certain functions should sit. Thank you for the opportunity to appear before you today. Let me start with a simple proposition. If New Zealand wants affordable and reliable electricity while we electrify transport, industry, and homes, and while we build resilience to storms and climate impacts, then our planning system needs to treat electricity networks as the essential enabling infrastructure that they are.

Activities of electricity networks are neither occasional nor exceptional. They are so routine and so embedded in daily life that when they are working well, they should largely happen in the background. That is sensible planning. It saves customers money, speeds up delivery, and allows communities to grow without unnecessary friction. When the planning system does not keep pace with that reality, the impacts show up quickly.

Delayed housing connections, slower upgrades, higher costs for consumers, and reduced resilience for communities. Vector's perspective comes from operating infrastructure that is present everywhere, along streets, through suburbs, rural areas, and sensitive environments. We have electricity assets on every street in Auckland. Electricity distribution is foundational to almost every home and business, and underpins many of the development outcomes the planning system is trying to achieve.

Vector is New Zealand's largest electricity distributor. We supply more than 632,000 electricity connections across Auckland from Papakura to Wellsford through a network of nearly 20,000 kilometers of lines and cables. Put simply, our network is longer than the distance from Auckland to London. That scale matters. Thousands of maintenance, replacement, upgrade, and repair activities occur every year just to keep the system safe, reliable, and ready for growth and electrification.

Because that work is so routine and so widespread, even small amounts of regulatory friction multiply rapidly across the network and ultimately flow through to customers and communities. Vector supports the government's intent to replace the RMA with a system that is faster, clearer, and more consistent, fewer plans, fewer consents, less duplication, and faster resolution.

Those objectives are absolutely the right direction for enabling infrastructure delivery at the pace and scale that New Zealand needs. Our submission focuses on a small number of targeted improvements to ensure the new system delivers three outcomes that matter to New Zealanders: safety, certainty, and system coherence.

Those outcomes are the lens that we ask the committee to apply. Firstly, on national direction, we support the bill's top-down architecture aimed at achieving national consistency. But that architecture will only work efficiently if national direction is sufficiently clear about how conflicts between different national instruments are resolved.Electricity networks, because of their extensive and linear nature, inevitably intersect with areas affected by other national policy objectives, such as biodiversity or freshwater

protection. If those conflicts are not clearly resolved at the national level, they tend to be re-litigated repeatedly through local plans and consent processes. That creates delay, uncertainty, and cost for infrastructure that communities rely on every day. Our recommendation is therefore to ensure National Direction operates as a genuinely cross-cutting framework that clearly resolves conflicts between instruments and that recognizes the role of essential infrastructure in supporting the land use outcomes the system is designed to

enable. Secondly, on the National Direction pipeline. Electricity networks have historically been affected by national policy created in other sectors without sufficient consideration of operational requirements. Our submission points to the NPS on urban development, where higher density outcomes have, in some cases, resulted in buildings being constructed dangerously close to overhead lines, contrary to the mandatory safe distance rules set out in ECP thirty-four. That example illustrates why infrastructure safety and operability need to be considered early in the national policy

process. We therefore recommend a requirement for practical pre-notification engagement with core infrastructure providers when national instruments are being developed. Early input can identify drafting issues and unintended consequences before policies are finalized. We also recommend allowing sufficient submission time on the first tranche of national instruments, extending the proposed twenty working days to forty, so stakeholders can provide meaningful, meaningful input on complex system-wide settings. Thirdly, on infrastructure

enablement in the planning system itself. A recurring ins- issue under the current system has been routine electricity network maintenance becoming caught in disproportionate planning processes. This is exactly the kind of low impact, well-understood work that should be able to proceed efficiently in the background to keep the lights on. Current examples in Auckland show why this matters. We currently have four consent applications in with Auckland Council simply to replace end-of-life power poles. These replacements are required for safety and reliability as

part of normal asset management. Because the new poles are slightly more than ten percent taller than the existing poles and are located within a height sensitive overlay, the activity is classified as non-complying under the current plan and must be publicly notified. The result is a clear mismatch between effects and process, with the process likely to cost more than the work itself. We have already committed about eighty thousand dollars in consent deposits, and we expect an additional thirty to forty thousand dollars in planning, technical, and engagement costs for these routine

replacements. In total, we expect the process will cost around a hundred and twenty thousand dollars for works that themselves are worth roughly a hundred thousand dollars. Meanwhile, in the same area, Auckland Transport can install new light poles up to twenty-five meters high as a permitted activity. This example illustrates how routine infrastructure maintenance can become trapped in administrative process despite having minimal environmental effects, and it doesn't stop there.

In the same area as those just lodged pole consents, we yesterday became aware of a customer connection job that will trigger another publicly notified consent. That includes a replacement pole that again breaches the ten percent height threshold and a new transformer in the berm that breaches the nine hundred millimeter height limit for new assets. These are standard low impact network components. But because of how the thresholds apply, that single connection is now at risk of being delayed by several months, and that connection services a

twenty-six unit housing development. So the practical outcome of this settings mismatch is not just extra process and cost for the network operator, it is real delay for new homes getting connected to electricity for works with negligible effects. That is exactly the kind of outcome this reform is trying to avoid. Our recommendation is therefore that the new system ensures routine network maintenance and replacement can proceed as permitted activities where effects are low, with public notification reserved for genuinely significant impacts.

Finally, turning briefly to the Natural Environment Bill. Essential infrastructure will inevitably interact with environmentally constrained areas from time to time, and the system needs a clear pathway to manage those effects without making critical infrastructure impossible. The bill appropriately allows a consenting pathway where infrastructure may interact with environmental limits.

However, our submission recommends removing the qualifier "significant" from that pathway as it introduces ambiguity and invites unnecessary dispute. Finally, we raise a practical issue regarding long-lived infrastructure in the coastal marine area, such as submarine electricity cables that provide enduring services to communities.

Fixed maximum consent durations for these assets may create unnecessary re-consenting costs without improving environmental outcomes.
